data.title: Is USOI A Winner? Unveiling The Income Potential And Risks Of This Oil ETN
data.description:
The Credit Suisse X-Links Crude Oil Shares Covered Call ETN (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:USOI) offers investors a high monthly distribution through its covered call strategy, which is executed on the United States Oil Fund (USO). This exchange-traded note (ETN) allows investors to earn significant income from oil market exposure, while giving up upside beyond a set strike price. USOI is designed to take advantage of stable or slightly rising oil prices by selling call options that are 6% out-of-the-money, providing consistent cash flow.

However, investors must consider the risks associated with USOI’s structure, including tracking errors, reduced income during periods of low oil volatility, and the credit risk associated with the issuing bank, UBS AG. This article delves into USOI's investment strategy, performance, and whether it remains an attractive option for investors seeking income from oil market exposure.

Fund Overview and Investment Strategy

The fund has been created since April 25th, 2017 and currently has net assets of $347.5 million. The price action of USOI gives it a beta of 0.57, resulting in a relatively low volatility profile in comparison with the volatility of the S&P 500 Stock Index (SPY).

USOI follows the performance of the Credit Suisse Nasdaq WTI Crude Oil FLOWS 106 Index. This index tracks the performance of a covered call strategy on the United States Oil Fund (USO), an ETF that invests in WTI crude oil futures. The covered call strategy involves selling call options on the USO ETF, with a strike price set at 106% of the underlying price, or 6% out of the money (OTM). The premiums collected from selling these options are distributed to USOI investors as monthly income (similar to dividend payments to shareholders of a certain stock).

The strategy behind USOI is relatively simple: by holding a long position in the USO and selling call options, USOI generates consistent income while capping the upside potential of its underlying oil position. Investors earn monthly premiums but give up any gains beyond the 6% OTM strike price. Importantly, while this strategy provides cash flow, it offers no downside protection beyond the premiums received. If the price of oil declines significantly, USOI's performance will follow the underlying USO ETF's losses.

Its objective is to generate consistent cash flow for investors in exchange for limiting the upside on oil prices. In an ideal world, we would prefer to see the underlying price of USO either rise steadily upward, or be range-bound. In one of these scenarios, USOI should be able to offer a decent ROI to its investors. We do want to point out USOI charges a 0.85% annual expense ratio, making it a relatively expensive product for oil investors, particularly when compared to simpler commodity ETFs.

Additionally, as an ETN, USOI represents a debt obligation from UBS AG, meaning investors face credit risk if the issuer encounters financial difficulties. As UBS AG currently holds a strong A+ rating, we will not delve into the details of the potential credit risk here.

Performance and Yields

Oil prices have been going through a volatile year in the past 12 months. In the past year, USO declined with 5.1% whereas USOI lost 7.11%. The losses of USO can be explained by the price evolution of Crude Oil Futures (CL1:COM), which lost roughly 19% is the past year.

It's important to notice here that USO tracks the price of West Texas intermediate (WTI) crude oil futures. It is the largest ETF to track WTI crude oil future, with total assets of $1.3 billion under management. USO does not purchase physical barrels of oil, but invests in futures contracts, which are a derivative agreement to buy or sell crude oil at a future date. These futures have different maturity dates, and USO's performance is closely tied to the performance of the futures in the near-month. As the futures contracts expire every month, USO needs to "roll" the contracts regularly (by selling soon-to-expire contracts and buy contracts which expire later in the future).

When oil futures are in contango (where longer-dated futures are more expensive than near-term contracts), the ETF loses value due to the cost of rolling contracts. Fortunately, the current oil market is in backwardation, meaning that USOI benefits from a positive "roll yield" when selling more expensive near-term contracts and buying cheaper longer-term ones. This has been the case for WTI futures for the past months now, and it explains why USO delivered a better return than the CL futures did.

In exchange for giving up the upside on the USO ETF, USOI investors receive an attractive monthly distribution (similar to a dividend), with a trailing 12-month distribution yield of 20.81%. It's important to be aware this dividend yield can fluctuate throughout the year and there is no guarantee it will maintain at this current 20% level.

In summary, while USOI offers impressive short-term returns and yields, it is still bound by the performance of the underlying crude oil futures. An investment in USOI would have outperformed a similar investment in USO or CL futures, but you would still have lost money. This underperformance can be attributed to USOI’s reliance on the USO ETF, which holds oil futures contracts that are rolled monthly.

Market Outlook

In order to judge the future prospect of USOI, we need to anticipate multiple facets.

Oil prices in 2024 are at a crucial turning point. On one hand, geopolitical tensions, particularly in Ukraine and the Middle East, have supported oil prices. OPEC+ production cuts have also played a significant role in keeping oil prices elevated. On the other hand, a slowing global economy, particularly in China, has dampened demand for oil. This creates a precarious balance, with oil prices caught between conflicting bullish and bearish forces. Recently, both HSBC and Macquarie have lowered their forecasts for the oil price in 2025 and based on their analysis, I tend to agree with their view.

We also need to take into consideration the overall level of implied volatility in the oil futures markets. The total gain from the covered call positions will be directly tied to the value of the sold call options, which is impacted by the underlying implied volatility of the crude oil futures.

Here we can see the implied volatility has recently risen to new high levels, which should have a positive impact on the value of the sold call options.

A final factor we need to keep in mind is the state of backwardation. As this state creates positive "roll yields" for USO, we can expect this to have a positive impact on USOI going forward.

Risks & considerations

Investors considering USOI should be aware of several risks. First, as an ETN, USOI is a debt instrument issued by UBS AG. Investors do not own any physical oil or futures contracts, but rather are exposed to the performance of the index through a contractual obligation with UBS. While we deem this risk to be minor, we do need to take it into consideration.

The main risk to take into consideration is the price of the crude oil futures, which has been volatile throughout 2024, following the impact of the global events (war in the Middle East, war in Ukraine, lower demand in China, ...). The option strategy of USOI does not protect investors against sharp declines in the price of future crude oil contracts.

Another risk is the performance of oil futures and the impact of market structure on USOI’s returns. The USO ETF, which underlies USOI, gains exposure to oil through the futures market, meaning it is vulnerable to issues like contango. During periods of contango, USO’s value erodes as it rolls over futures contracts, leading to long-term underperformance. Fortunately, the current oil market is in backwardation, which benefits USOI by allowing it to profit from roll yields.

Another risk is the level of implied volatility is crucial for USOI's income generation, as higher volatility increases the premiums collected from selling call options. With implied oil volatility at its lowest levels since 2018, the premiums received from selling options have been significantly reduced. This makes it harder for USOI to sustain its high distribution yield. If volatility remains low, investors could see a decline in the ETN’s monthly payouts.

Conclusion

For income-focused investors looking for exposure to oil without betting on a strong price rally, USOI may be an attractive option. The ETN’s covered call strategy allows it to generate cash income, particularly in periods of stable or moderately rising oil prices. The current backwardation in the oil futures market provides a tailwind for USOI, supporting its roll yield and helping to offset the limitations of its call-writing strategy.

However, USOI’s long-term underperformance, driven by tracking errors in the USO ETF and periods of low oil volatility, makes it a less appealing choice for investors with a longer time horizon. Given the current low volatility environment, investors should closely monitor market conditions, as a prolonged period of low volatility could lead to reduced monthly distributions.

Overall, USOI is best suited for tactical use by income-seeking investors who are comfortable with the risks of futures-based oil exposure and the limitations of the covered call strategy. Investors should remain cautious and be prepared for potential downside if bearish factors in the oil market begin to dominate.

data.title: Enphase Q3 Recap: This Time, America Is Not The Problem (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

American solar energy technology company Enphase (NASDAQ:ENPH) reported their Q3 report, which showed revenue underwhelmed due to slower shipments of microinverters and battery megawatts.

The Fremont, CA-headquartered company has held its head above stormy water, enduring a difficult high-interest rate environment as some of its competitors still find it challenging. Still, Enphase continues to navigate a challenging turnaround for the company as competition and tough macro environments pressurize the company’s growth.

Throughout Enphase’s attempted turnaround in the past few months, the company expressed the challenges it was navigating from the American market, particularly in California. But the Q3 report showed that international revenues are falling off a cliff, with weakness in Europe adding to Enphase’s woes.

This report will add some more pressure on Enphase, with the stock already down 30% for the year before the Q3 report.

I had been bullish on Enphase, but the updates in the company's Q3 report indicate more work needs to be done before I can be bullish again. For now, I recommend a Hold rating on Enphase.

In Enphase’s Q3, it's now Europe’s turn to be a thorn

I had written about Enphase in June this year, where I expressed my confidence in the company due to its strong focus on profitability in a challenging environment for solar stocks.

My conviction in its profitability outlook stemmed from the company’s improving inventory levels, which would lower its cost profile even further.

In Q3, Enphase reported revenues of $381 million, down 31% y/y pointing to smaller revenue declines but still missing Street's estimates of ~$393 million. A review of the company’s financial metrics that I periodically look at showed inventory levels stabilized further while COGS improved as well. On the call to discuss earnings, management said that inventory levels were improving at the factory as well as in the sales and distribution channels. But part of the improvement was also due to channel restocking in Q3, which management does not expect to see the benefits of in Q4.

The Americas region, the most important business region for Enphase, saw revenues grow sequentially 43% to $284 million, accounting for 3/4th of Enphase’s consolidated revenues per the company’s updated quarterly filings. These results showed improvement in the US markets versus last year. Management hinted at improving distributor channel dynamics by revealing that sell-through, a key channel sales metric to measure inventory sold to end customers, rose 6% y/y in Q3 across all sales channels in the US.

Particularly in California, sell-through rose 13% as NEM 3.0 became the new norm for the solar industry in the state. Management said that they shipped 1.2 million microinverters from their US manufacturing facilities versus the global microinverters capacity of ~7.25 million, which still points to some weakness in the global solar markets.

While the US showed some signs of improving, Europe turned out to be a thorn in Enphase’s Q3 report, with revenue growth dropping ~52% y/y to ~$97 million.

Even sequentially, revenue growth was down 15%, which does not look too good on Enphase’s Q3 report. Many key European markets, such as the Netherlands, France, and Germany, were either impacted by regulatory uncertainty, weather seasonality, or tepid economic growth. According to management, “economic growth is slow and consumer confidence is limited” in Europe. Management noted that sell-through rates in the European region fell 34% y/y.

Management looks to launch more products in Europe to stir up demand and position themselves for a strong 2025. Enphase’s 2-Gen IQ EV Chargers, which are already shipping in the Americas, are soon to be shipped to European markets as well. I don’t expect this to be a major catalyst just yet, since EV chargers in European markets are not growing as fast as the 34% growth that the US markets are growing at. Still, product refreshes might help, according to management. In addition to EV chargers, the company is also shipping 3rd gen 5P IQ batteries to more markets in Europe, with shipments in the Americas well under way.

Enphase’s Q3 Results Bring Questions On Profitability

In Q3, Enphase’s earnings fell by more than half to $45.76 million, or 33 cents a share, from $113.95 million, or 80 cents a share, a year earlier. Margins across the board contracted, with gross margins contracting by 70 bps to 46.8% while operating margins contracted to 13% from 21.4% over the same time period. On a sequential basis, however, margins expanded.

Per their quarterly filings, Enphase’s debt levels dropped 8% to $1.2 billion versus $1.3 billion at the start of the year. Cash, equivalents, and marketable securities were slightly up at $1.76 million.

Enphase Outlook: Turning cautious on near-term guidance

I am turning cautious on Enphase because of challenges it sees in the European markets, as well as some possible peer pressures on its EV charger business.

Management mentioned they expect “revenue for Q4 to be within a range of $360 million to $400 million, which includes shipments of 140-megawatt to 160-megawatt hours of IQ Batteries,” which is sequentially below the 173 megawatt hours of IQ Batteries shipped in Q3. Further, management strongly cautioned at persistent weakness for Europe in Q4 and said they would be under-shipping and said that Q4 guidance “incorporates a slowdown in Europe in Q4.”

Management has not indicated when they expect any turnaround in the European markets yet. Based on these weaknesses, I expect 2025 revenues to be ~1.84 billion under what current street estimates are while also dropping my EBITDA estimates for Enphase to 15% growth next year, down from the near-20% growth I initially expected.

These growth rates suggest Enphase be valued at 22x 2025 earnings, which indicates ~10% upside or a PT of ~$101.

Risks & Other Factors To Look For

In addition to the weakness in European markets that I pointed out through this research note, I would also be closely watching the US markets for any weakness. Microinverters & battery shipments show sequential normalization, but any further weakness would strain Enphase’s outlook.

Further, the interest rate environment looks muddled again with a rapid rise in the interest rates since mid-September. I do not expect significant increases in interest rates from here, but any change in the narrative would impact not just Enphase but the whole solar industry.

Takeaway

Enphase’s Q3 results underwhelmed expectations with smaller battery and microinverter shipments than expected, resulting in the solar energy company posting revenue growth that was below the bar.

Weakness in European markets impacted Enphase’s results, and with no improvement in sight, the impact is expected to linger around, causing some headwinds to Enphase’s growth.

I have reduced my price targets to Enphase and downgraded the company to a Hold.

data.title: Energy Vault: Upgrading To A Buy As Delisting Risk Abates
data.description:
Four months ago, I published my first piece on energy storage solutions developer Energy Vault Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:NRGV) wherein I rated the stock a Hold on several grounds. In the article, I pointed out one big reason why I did not feel NRGV was a good buy at the time was the stock’s negative momentum that put it in danger of turning into a penny stock and being kicked off the NYSE. Sure enough, last month, Energy Vault revealed it had been served a notice by the NYSE for non-compliance with the exchange’s continued listing standards after NRGV traded below $1 over a consecutive 30 trading-day period. Under NYSE rules, Energy Vault had a period of six months from the time of the notice to regain compliance with minimum stock price listing requirements.

When Energy Vault announced it would consider available alternatives to cure the stock price non-compliance, my first guess was that a reverse stock split was imminent. After all, since 2010, over 900 companies have reverse split, most of which were companies priced under $1. To be fair, reverse splits tend to be quite effective, leading to improved liquidity (value traded), tighter spreads, shorter queues, less price volatility and less dark (off-exchange) trading. However, I would only consider upgrading NRGV to Buy from Hold if its business prospects improved significantly (more of that later). Thankfully, Energy Vault might not need one, after all. NRGV rocketed 24.5% in Monday’s session after the company announced it has signed a $350M agreement with Houston, Texas-based Enervest Group to deploy a 1 GWh battery energy storage system (BESS) at the Stoney Creek site in Australia. The BESS system will provide flexible, grid-scale energy storage and enhance the stability of the New South Wales power grid. The giant battery storage system will be built with Energy Vault’s proprietary UL9540 and AS3000 certified B-VAULT product, coupled with Vault-OS Energy Management System to control, manage, and optimize its operations.

The latest share surge removes NRGV from danger of being delisted from the NYSE. However, the most important part about the Enervest deal is that it greatly improves Energy Vault’s revenue visibility. As I pointed out in the June article, shrinking revenues are the biggest reason why NRGV shares have performed poorly since its February 2022 IPO, despite the company making decent progress in a promising niche. For the first quarter, Energy Vault reported revenue of $7.76M (-32.0% Y/Y), $5.53M below the Wall Street consensus while GAAP EPS of -$0.14 was in-line. Adjusted EBITDA improved $4.6 million or 24% year-over-year to -$14.4 million from -$19.0 million, mainly due to lower operating expenses while cash OpEx of $16.7 million improved 22% year-over-year and 14% quarter-over-quarter due to cost-side measures undertaken in Q4 2023. The second quarter was not any better: Q2 GAAP EPS of -$0.18 missed by $0.06 while Q2 revenue of $3.8M (-90.4% Y/Y) missed by $10.98M. The company reaffirmed its full-year 2024 guidance for revenue, gross margin, adjusted EBITDA and year-end cash balance that it issued during its Investor & Analyst Day Presentation held on May 9th. The company expects full-year revenue in the $50M-$100M range, good for 70-85% Y/Y decline after posting $341 million in 2023 which the company attributed to the timing of revenue associated with licenses for its gravity energy storage technology.

Energy Vault’s longer-term outlook appears much better than the short-term outlook. The company expects revenue for 2024-2025 revenue to clock in at $500-$700M, good for 23% growth after realizing $487M in 2022-2023. This points to a sharp revenue ramp in 2025 considering the company expects to bag not more than $100M revenue in the current year. Gross margin guidance for 2024-2025 was 15%-20% compared to 16% for the previous two years. The company has ample growth runways considering it has a backlog of 710 MWh worth $225M contracted and/or in progress. The company describes Developed Pipeline as short-listed or awarded projects at gross contract value.

In the previous article, I discussed, in detail, Energy Vault’s grid-scale gravity energy storage systems (GESS). This company operates under 4 segments:

  • G-Vault–Gravity storage systems

  • B-Vault– Battery storage

  • H-Vault– Hybrid and hydrogen storage

  • Software & Services

I’m more excited about Energy Vault’s gravity storage systems because the company can build a moat around its proprietary GESS systems that help differentiate it from rivals such as Eos Energy Enterprises (EOSE), Energy Dome, and Energy Storage Systems. Last year, Energy Vault successfully commissioned the world’s first grid-scale gravity energy storage system (GESS) that does not use water in China. The 25MW/100MWh EVx GESS is located outside of Shanghai in Rudong, Jiangsu Province, sited adjacent to a wind farm and a national grid interconnection site with a view to balancing the country’s national energy grid through long-duration renewable energy storage.

Energy Vault can make money from its G-Vault segment through licensing, build/transfer and own/operate. The company has already booked $100M in Gravity IP licenses over the next 10 years, with $200M+ of potential volume royalties over the next 10 years. However, I think its B-Vault segment including its BESS products (such as the Enervest project) will be crucial in getting this 7-year old company off the ground, despite having no licensing opportunities and its fatter margins. According to the company, the battery storage segment has a $2.7B developed pipeline (9.9GWh) for potential conversion over the next 12 - 24 months.

Takeaway

NRGV shares are giving up some of the big gains in Thursday’s session. However, the shares are currently trading above $1.80, way higher than the share price of $1.02 when I published the June piece. The 14-day RSI reading of 81 suggests this stock is in overbought territory, something that might limit near-term gains. However, one thing I have learned with beaten down companies is that the shares can build huge momentum that sometimes trumps technicals when they begin to show solid turnaround potential. One such company is Power Solutions International (OTCPK:PSIX), with the shares nearly quadrupling from the time I published a bullish piece in June.

With a current market cap at $282M, Energy Vault shares are trading at just 1.3 times current backlog, suggesting they are undervalued. The company has $2.7B in developed BESS pipeline for potential conversion over the next 12 - 24 months, while the initial results for its GESS Chinese projects have been very encouraging. I believe Energy Vault’s GESS products are very promising and will help the company differentiate from its storage rivals; however, the company’s BESS products are critical in driving top line growth and buy the company time for its gravity storage segment to gain real traction.

In the June article, nearly 20% of Energy Vault shares were sold short at the time with 17 days to cover, presenting an opportunity for a nice short squeeze. That has declined to around 10% but with nearly 16 days to cover. Wall Street’s consensus is that Energy Vault is on the verge of breakeven, with the company set to post a final loss in 2025, before becoming profitable in 2026. Based on 4 Wall Street analysts, Energy Vault has an average price target of $2.50, still good for 35% upside from current price. I find this price target reasonable, with a fair chance for even bigger gains as the company continues to monetize its considerable backlog. I now rate NRGV a Buy.

data.title: The 6% Yield DIF Portfolio Outperformed In 2024, I'm Changing It For 2025
data.description:
Introduction

This month marks the first full year of my coverage being published to Seeking Alpha. Last year, I published my fixed income portfolio, the Diversified Income Fund, or "DIF" for short.

For those unaware, this is a reference to gamer slang.

The portfolio is designed to produce current income and aims for capital preservation as a secondary objective.

Meet the Old DIF

Here is the old portfolio, without the changes I've made for this year. This is just for context, to show you where we came from.

From the date of publication of that article, here is how the portfolio performed without DRIP (does account for dividends paid, but assumes they are not reinvested) against its benchmarks, the aggregate bond market, the 7-10yr treasuries ETF. It outperformed its benchmarks in total return and in volatility management.

The portfolio did not do any rebalancing during this time. Here is how the assets drifted. The biggest winners were our emerging markets mutual funds, and our only losers were the leveraged treasuries. That thesis hasn't fully played out yet, as we are waiting on the Fed to continue lowering rates, which, I believe, will continue as we move into 2025. For that reason, these positions will stay as-is in the DIF model while we wait for the Fed.

I wrote about the TYA ETF recently in regards to the 10yr UST. Check it out if you want more info on the thesis.

From the first article's publication (12/28/23) until the time of writing (10/22/24), the portfolio has experienced about 1% of NAV as a capital loss and has been paid about 4.8% of NAV in dividends. This renders a total return of 3.8% so far. DIF is still projected to return a total yield of 6.3% by the end of the year.

After this article is published, I will rebalance this portfolio and create the new model to run alongside it. This will allow us to see if the changes we make for 2025 are worthwhile.

Portfolio Goals

DIF has several goals that I've set out for it:

  • Achieve >6% yield
  • Overall duration of ≤7yr
  • Favorable risk/return metrics compared to:
    • Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)
    • 7-10yr US Treasury Index (IEF)
  • Composed primarily of high quality fixed income instruments, but may include up to the following allocations:
    • 25% Non-US Credit
    • 25% Below-IG Credit
    • 15% Alternative Strategies & Derivatives
  • Has an annualized standard deviation ("stdev") of ≤10%, & a beta of ≤0.5

Portfolio Themes

DIF invests across themes, mostly macroeconomic or geopolitical in nature. These are fundamental themes that inform the asset mix in the portfolio.

Here are the key themes from last year:

1) Taking advantage of the Fed's dovish turn on "peak rates," which I wrote about here. I am buying fixed rate bonds again, as discussed in that article in more detail.

2) There is now a divergence between the market's expectation of credit risk and economic indices. I see asymmetric upside in lower and unrated credit, further down in the "corporate stack" and have added to allocations of BBB-B CLOs and senior loans.

3) Emerging and developing market bonds are currently offering incredibly compelling yields for the risk involved, but I prefer active management in this sector to cover my ignorance of ex-US macroeconomics.

The Fed did indeed make a pivot on rates this year, validating the first theme and my purchase of fixed-rate products, leveraged treasuries, and holding my cash in short-term treasuries.

The second theme is still in effect, and one that I am going to add to in this year's changes.

The third theme is also still in effect, now bolstered by geopolitical tensions flaring up at key global economic choke points. This has added risk to the emergent markets theme. However, I will not be reducing exposure for the coming year as the funds DIF holds in emerging markets are actively managed toward countries with more stability outside of current conflicts.

Beware of passive emerging market indices that may not make these distinctions and could add risk to your portfolio.

Introducing the New DIF

Without further ado, here is the new, updated portfolio:

Ticker Fund Weight
KORP American Century Diversified Corporate Bond ETF 10%
BOND

Active Bond Exchange-Traded Fund

 10%
DCARX DFA California Municipal Real Return Portfolio Fund 20%
TYA Simplify Intermediate Term Treasury Futures Strategy ETF 8%
TUA Simplify Short-Term Treasury Futures Strategy ETF 12%
JAAA Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF 12%
JBBB Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF 8%
AGEPX American Beacon Developing World Income Fund 5%
DLENX DoubleLine Emerging Markets Fixed Income Fund 5%
SVOL Simplify Volatility Premium ETF 10%

Asset Breakdown

  • KORP & BOND: Broad bond beta, focus on corporates
  • DCARX: California infrastructure municipal bonds
  • TYA & TUA: Leveraged treasuries
  • JAAA & JBBB: Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs)
  • AGEPX & DLENX: Emerging market bonds
  • SVOL: Short volatility strategy, derivative income

The Major Changes

Goodbye LQD, Hello KORP & BOND

The first change was getting rid of LQD. After watching how well actively managed bond funds performed in the portfolio, it felt odd to have a passive index of older, lower yielding bonds. This allocation has now been split between the American Century Diversified Corporate Bond ETF (KROP) and the PIMCO Active Bond ETF (BOND), to give some diversity of managers to the strategy, while still getting 20% exposure to corporate bond beta and yield.

The primary reason to add active management is to avoid getting lost in the bond regime change. Passive indices hold a ton of debt that was issued in 2020-2021, when ZIRP was strictly in effect, and rates were very low. Active managers will be able to filter these bonds out and roll them into higher yielding, more current issues sooner and take advantage of these rates longer than a passive index that will wait until it is scheduled to roll over regardless of the Fed's choices.

Both funds have shown their prowess this year, outperforming the passive LQD index YTD in both upside and downside volatility.

They also both have higher yields, which pulls the total yield of the portfolio up very slightly.

Goodbye SEIX, Hello CLOs

The Virtus Seix Senior Loan ETF gave us access to leveraged buyout loans, but ultimately failed to impress me in this performance, given the performance of less risky assets in the "capital stack" like CLOs. CLOs, on the other hand, very much impressed me this year. I was very happy with this allocation, and I am going to increase it moving forward.

CLOs offer great diversification benefits from their internal components, and CLO funds magnify this by bundling hundreds of CLOs together. Both the Janus Henderson funds that DIF held last year performed very well, and so they are going to take on SEIX's weight as it exits the portfolio.

Comparison

So how do these two stack up now that changes have been made? I'm already looking forward to next year's update.

Here are the major stats for each portfolio:

Metric Old DIF New DIF
Yield 6.30% 6.31%
Duration 5.54 4.65
Sharpe 0.45 0.60
Sortino 0.63 0.87
Stdev 3.97% 3.65%
Net ER 0.44% 0.48%

While ER is slightly up, Stdev is down, risk and return metrics are up, duration is down, and yield is very slightly up. Overall, these look like positive changes to me.

Performance

The New DIF, as the data suggests, is less volatile than the Old DIF. It would've been a better choice for this year (and I believe will be for next year too!). Here is their total return against the benchmark.

Beta Exposure

When looking at styles, we can see that the portfolio moved from being heavy on corporate bond beta to a significantly lower amount, despite the fact that corporate bond weighting stayed the same. This is due to the active management of KORP and BOND diversifying their funds. We can see that there is also more exposure to short-term treasury beta as well.

DCARX Warning

As a reminder from my original article, DCARX is a mutual fund that is a few things:

  1. Only a great deal for CA natives like myself (since I do not pay taxes on the income from the fund, but you may in your state)
  2. It is difficult to get access
    1. I have access because of my profession
  3. The returns may look low in backtesting because they do not account for tax savings.

Conclusion

The DIF portfolio got a facelift, and is ready for 2025. While its core themes did not change, I altered how the portfolio is working toward profiting from those themes by consolidating my exposure away from corporate bonds and senior debt into actively managed US bonds and CLOs. These changes make the portfolio look more resilient in backtesting, have more favorable risk to reward metrics, and will overall help the portfolio achieve its stated goals.

This is a portfolio designed for myself as an income generator, but may not be the best for you. If anything needs tweaking for your situation, it's likely DCARX, but it is the best fund I've found for myself.

Suitability wise, I would recommend this portfolio to the retiree who has "won the game and can stop playing," meaning that they are happy to pull income from the portfolio at a high rate, 6%, but are willing to forgo much capital appreciation. While this portfolio should be self-sustaining and not draw itself down too badly, there is a possibility that the portfolio will experience capital depreciation, like we saw with the TYA and TUA positions. I expect these to turn around, but there are no guarantees.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: What's Going On? Take A Look At The Price Of Gold
data.description:
What is going on?

There are calls for people to be more aware of some of the things that are happening.

Just look at the article by Mohamed El-Eriian in the Financial Times:

"Something strange has happened to the price of gold over the past year. In setting one record level after the other, it seems to have decoupled from its traditional historical influencers, such as interest rates, inflation, and the dollar."

Take a look at the performance of the price of gold over the past 12 months.

Gold Continuous Contract GC00 (U.S.:Nymex)

The price of this Gold Continuous Contract (GC00) rose from $1,986.10 on October 24, 2023, to $2,758.30 on October 22, 2024. This represents a rise of almost 39 percent...in just one year.

Mr. El-Erian points to the fact that other asset prices have followed a similar path over the same period of time.

Here are the data from the S&P 500 stock index over the past 12 months. Note that the S&P 500 stock index has been setting one record after another as it has climbed through the year.

S&P 500 Stock Index

The S&P 500 stock index rose from 4,103.78 on October 27, 2023, to 5855.09 on October 22, 2024, a rise of 42.7 percent.

Is this just a coincidence...or, is there something else going on here?

Mr. El-Erian just dismisses this by saying that the "correlation itself is unusual."

Just one further note here because I keep coming back to this over and over again.

During this full 12-month time period, the Federal Reserve was conducting a policy of "quantitative easing." In fact, the Fed's effort at quantitative tightening actually began in the middle of March 2022...more than two years ago.

Where did all the dollars come from to result in a 42.7 percent rise in stock prices and a 39.0 percent rise in the price of gold?

Before getting to this, let me just introduce one other piece of information.

Brooke Masters just released another bit of news in the Financial Times reporting that Gary Gensler, chair of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, stated in a speech given on Tuesday that the amount of uninsured U.S. dollars held offshore in non-U.S. banks amounted to $13 trillion!

Ms. Masters then states that the number of dollars held in domestic U.S. banks and credit unions totalled $20 trillion.

I point to this number to show just how many dollars are afloat in the world!

Mr. Gensler point is that what happens to these monies could cause "the global regulatory community" some real trouble at some time in the near future.

Mr. Gensler makes mention of the fact that "Rapid outflows of uninsured deposits were a key source of stress in the 2023 U.S. regional banking crisis."

So, as Mr. El-Erian warns us...something strange is going on in the world.

The amount of U.S. dollars afloat in the world is enormous.

The effort of the Federal Reserve to protect the United States...and the world...from the spread of the Covid-19 pandemic...and from the recession that followed...left the United States...and the world...with an excessive amount of liquidity.

We see that this liquidity has flowed all over the world.

And, we see that these funds are helping to keep the U.S. stock market going...and the price of gold rising...and other things throughout the globe.

On top of this, the United States has $35 trillion in government debt outstanding, and it looks as if, regardless of who is elected the President of the United States in two weeks, the amount of U.S. debt outstanding will hit $40 trillion sometime in the next four years.

Something different is going on in the world.

The United States has flooded the world with U.S. dollars.

Markets are not behaving as they have in the past.

Geopolitics, as Mr. El-Erian suggests, is not what it once was.

All this is why, things don't seem to "match-up" the way they once did.

Mr. El-Erian suggests that "The West Must Pay More Attention To Gold Price's Jump."

In my view, the West must pay more attention to the major disequilibrium that exists in the world and is being called out by what is happening to the price of gold.

The unusual behavior of the price of gold points to something, something that the investment community must be cognizant of.

Things aren't the same.

But, that should make us uncomfortable.

data.title: FTEC: Eyes On Mag 7 Earnings, Momentum Is Back
data.description:
I came across a remarkable FactSet chart. It illustrates that earnings growth among the Magnificent 7 companies is essentially all of the S&P 500’s EPS rate of advance for the quarter just ended. We’ll see how it ultimately pans out, with mega-cap Information Technology firms reporting Q3 results later this month and into November.

As it stands, the I.T. chunk of the SPX trades near 30x earnings, which makes sense given that’s where the growth is. But we are likely to see a broadening out of the earnings-increase story in the quarters to come. The same FactSet note points out that the “Other 493” companies and sectors are set to produce EPS growth between 12% and 16% per quarter from the current period through Q4 2025.

I reiterate a hold rating on the Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FTEC). I pointed out back in the second quarter that upside momentum was very strong and that a technical move to $190 may have been in the cards, but I was also concerned about the valuation and an impending rally among value and cyclical stocks.

We saw that play out, but FTEC holders have enjoyed a big rally since early August, and the ETF is now among the leading areas of the S&P 500 since mid-September. I reiterate a hold rating today.

S&P 500 Q3 Earnings Growth: All About the Mag 7

S&P 500 Earnings Growth Set to Broaden Through YE 2025

According to the issuer, FTEC seeks to track the investment returns that correspond, before fees and expenses, generally to the performance of the MSCI USA IMI Information Technology 25/50 Index. The index is comprised of companies operating across information technology sectors. FTEC invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalization.

FTEC is a large ETF and has attracted new money in the last handful of months since I last reviewed the product. Assets under management are now $12.5 billion, up from $10 billion in Q2. With a low 0.08% annual expense ratio and a modest 0.62% annual dividend, it’s an ideal holding for long-term investors even in taxable accounts with the low distribution rate.

Share-price momentum has indeed been stellar since the spring – the ETF Grade per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system is an A+. FTEC also sports solid liquidity metrics, while its Risk rating is more mixed. The fund can feature elevated volatility at times, and the top three stocks account for nearly half of the fund. Average daily volume is north of 300,000 shares with a median 30-day bid/ask spread of three basis points, per Fidelity.

For a refresher on the portfolio, the 5-star, Silver-rated ETF by Morningstar has actually moved more toward the large-cap blend section of the style box in recent months. Of course, there remains a significant allocation to large-cap growth, while domestic SMID caps are a relatively small share of the fund.

What is about unchanged from the end of the first half of the year is FTEC’s price-to-earnings multiple, which remains just shy of the 29 handle. With long-term EPS growth of 13.7%, the resulting PEG ratio is no bargain at 2.1x.

FTEC: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

The big three stocks have moved around somewhat since June, but Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Microsoft (MSFT) still command a more than 40% chunk of the ETF.

In all, the top 10 holdings comprise 59% of FTEC, which is down a few percentage points from my last look. With earnings season heating up, it will be key to pay attention to both the fundamentals and technicals of AAPL, NVDA, and MSFT.

FTEC: Holdings & Dividend Information

Mag 7 Companies Earnings Dates

Seasonally, we are in the heart of a bullish stretch for big-cap US tech. October and November have been strong months when scanning return data over the past 10 years. December has seen mixed results, but then modest returns have generally been had in Q1 of the new year.

FTEC: Bullish October-November Trends

The Technical Take

Amid heightened volatility ahead of the uncertain election outcome, FTEC has performed well. Notice in the chart below that the fund is back to testing its July peak just above the $180 mark. The $190 target I outlined in the second quarter remains in play, and with a rising long-term 200-day moving average, the bulls appear in control of the primary trend. That assertion is backed up by a rising uptrend support line going back to early 2023 and considering that price is above both the 200dma and the shorter-term 50-day moving average.

But take a look at the RSI momentum gauge at the top of the graph – so far, it is printing a lower high as the fund encroaches on a fresh price peak. I’d like to see an RSI increase to help confirm a potential breakout heading into year-end. Downside support is likely to appear near $160 – the March high and where the 200dma currently comes into play. The $174 mark is a short-term support point too.

FTEC: $180 Resistance, Rising 200dma, $190 Target Still In Play

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on FTEC. The Information Technology sector ETF continues to sport strong momentum and technical strength, but with a PEG ratio above 2, there’s a significant valuation premium.

data.title: The Value Of Diversification: Insights From Our Q3 Economic And Market Review
data.description:
Financial markets moved higher yet again in the third quarter of 2024, and this time, everyone joined in! By that, I mean it didn't matter how you were invested - U.S. stocks, international stocks, emerging markets stocks, bonds, or real assets such as infrastructure and real estate investment trusts (REITs) - you did well.

This is the value of diversification, as our quarterly Economic and Market Review will clearly demonstrate. While a small group of stocks - the Magnificent Seven tech names - have dominated performance in the past few years, that was not the case in the third quarter. With inflation beginning to ease, the economy potentially slowing, and valuations for U.S. stocks easily outpacing their global peers, a new group of leaders emerged in the third quarter. Investors who hold diversified portfolios would have benefited.

Let's start our look back at the third quarter with U.S. stocks.

U.S. stocks (represented by the Russell 1000 Index) surged another 6% in the quarter ending Sept. 30, bringing the year-to-date return to 21.2%, the highest Q1-Q3 performance since 1997. Can they continue to rise for the rest of the year?

That's hard to say, but if history is any guide, it's worth noting that since 1984, there have been nine years in which Q1-Q3 U.S. stock returns have been above 20% - and only in one of those years was the Q4 return negative. That was in 1987 and was the result of the historic "Black Monday" crash in October that year.

What's even more noteworthy about this year's performance to date is that these strong returns have come in the face of weakened consumer sentiment due to ongoing inflation pressures, the upcoming presidential election and the accompanying headlines, an expanding war in the Middle East, two destructive hurricanes, and a port strike, among other issues.

Have the tables turned?

An interesting characteristic of the third quarter was that the usual suspects weren't the drivers of return. For the first half of the year, the Magnificent Seven technology names led performance globally, which meant that large U.S. growth stocks beat all others. The tables turned in the third quarter, with non-U.S. stocks beating U.S. stocks, small cap beating large cap, and value stocks beating growth stocks.

We've talked about needing more market breadth. The Magnificent Seven have so dominated performance in the past few years that by the end of June this year, they came to represent one-third of the S&P 500 Index. But that changed in the third quarter and U.S. large-cap stock performance was easily beaten by that of international stocks, emerging markets, and especially real assets. This is why we always emphasize diversification of your portfolio. And even though investors would have done well owning the Magnificent Seven over the past few years, it's never good to put all of your eggs in one basket, and you can never predict when the trend will reverse.

This is why you ideally own a lot of different things in a portfolio!

Stocks outside the U.S. are on sale

Non-U.S. equities have had lower valuations than U.S. equities for quite some time now, but the difference widened significantly this year. U.S. valuations are at the highest level they've been for several years, while international and emerging market stocks are at lower valuations. That means they are cheaper than U.S. stocks, even relative to history.

Meanwhile, their dividend yield is bigger. What's not to love? Non-U.S. stocks are not only on sale, they also pay out higher income. Moreover, the potential for the U.S. dollar to weaken as rates come down could help boost returns. Sounds like a win-win-win situation to me!

Even without these attractive attributes, including international and emerging markets stocks in a diversified portfolio helps give investors exposure to sectors and industries that aren't as prevalent in the U.S. Additionally, it's useful to remember that emerging markets represent 85% of the world population and 60% of the world's economy.

Another important change seen this year that became more noticeable in the third quarter is the reduced dominance of China in the emerging markets' universe. Before, investing in an emerging market index was placing a bet on China. Its dominance in those indexes has fallen now.

Ballast is back

When I said everything did well in the third quarter, I mean everything did well! And that goes for bonds, which have been kind of unloved in the past few years. The decline in interest rates in the quarter boosted the value of bonds. Meanwhile, investors holding bonds also enjoyed strong yields. Conservative investors who held on to their bonds through thick and thin are now seeing their payday.

Some of the recent disillusionment with bonds is that not only did yields fall significantly, their performance correlated with equities, so their primary role as a stable diversifier to stocks kind of went out the window. But it appears the ballast is back! And once again, it shows that holding a diversified portfolio is generally the best option for investors.

Interest rates go down, real assets go up

Real assets were among the biggest winners in the third quarter as interest rates fell. Lower rates have a double-barreled effect on real assets: they mean lower borrowing costs and the yields from real estate investment trusts (REITs) may start to look more attractive than bond yields.

I know there's been some reluctance to hold REITs due to the perception that they mainly represent commercial real estate - those downtown office towers that are still not at full capacity as we recover from the impact of the COVID pandemic. But when you look under the hood, a good percentage of U.S. and global REITs are composed of data centers, warehouses, health care facilities, retirement homes, and so on. And what can I say about infrastructure other than the world needs to build more roads, bridges, ports, airports and tunnels to keep the global economy running and to meet the needs of rapidly developing emerging markets like China and India.

Balanced portfolio holds out historically

What does this chart show? Well, for one, it shows the value of long-term investing. Asset class performance can vary significantly over a one-year horizon, but over five years, the majority of asset classes have historically posted positive performance, and the odds get greater the longer the period the assets are held. More importantly, however, a balanced portfolio has the best chance of positive performance over any period greater than five years.

And what else does this chart show? That no matter how old your clients are, they have lived through a decline in U.S. stocks of more than 20%. Most would have lived through a decline in U.S. stocks of more than 50%. In spite of that, the average return for a balanced portfolio over any rolling 10- or 20-year period was around 7%. This goes to show that investing success is fundamentally about time in the market, not timing the market.

The bottom line

There's a reason why we're perpetually hammering home the benefits of portfolio diversification and taking the long view in investing. It's because both strategies have proven, time and time again, to be critical components of a successful portfolio.

Look, market trends come and go. Asset prices ebb and flow. A balanced portfolio built for the long-term captures today's opportunities and manages tomorrow's risks. We're here to help along the way.

Disclosures

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer, solicitation or recommendation to purchase any security.

They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

data.title: Power Integrations: Emerging From The Downturn With GaN Growth On The Horizon
data.description:
At a time when investors are focused on AI chips, with companies like NVIDIA (NVDA) capturing most of the attention, analog semiconductor manufacturers have been underperforming their sector when using the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) as a proxy. For example, year-to-date Analog Devices (ADI) and Texas Instruments (TXN) have both underperformed SOXX, but some smaller analog semiconductor companies like Power Integrations (NASDAQ:POWI) have actually delivered negative returns despite attractive long-term prospects.

While analog chips tend to be considered less sexy, this can be favorable for investors. One attractive characteristic they have is that they tend to be more difficult to replace once added to a product's design, which means a design win can continue to generate revenue for years or even decades. For instance, Power Integrations has shared that close to half of its sales come from product families that were introduced before 2001.

As a reminder, Power Integrations specializes in highly advanced power conversion integrated circuits (ICs), which usually represent significantly more complex designs requiring multiple discrete analog parts. These ICs can be applied in energy production, transmission, and consumption. Products using their power chips range from electric bikes and scooters to mobile phone chargers, to solar and wind power inverters. They are also found in cars, HVAC systems, appliances like washing machines, laptops, and industrial motor controllers.

The last time we covered the company it was undergoing a severe inventory correction, but it finally appears to be coming out of it, and delivering positive revenue growth again. In the second quarter the company began recovering, with revenues increasing 16% sequentially, and with guidance for further gains in the third quarter. The inventory overhang has moderated significantly, clearing the way for a healthy recovery. The company reported it ended the June quarter with inventory at 7.8 weeks, a significant improvement from the peak it reached of 13.6 weeks in the third quarter of 2022. Another reason for cautious optimism is that GaN is expected to reach an inflection point in 2025, as Power Integrations migrates its portfolio from silicon to GaN based solutions.

Q2 Financial Results

Revenue for the second quarter came in at $106 million, and non-GAAP earnings were $0.28 per diluted share. Interestingly, the company saw revenue growth in its four end market categories. The revenue mix was 42% consumer, 33% industrial, 14% computer and 11% communications.

Power Integration's non-GAAP gross margin remains at a healthy level, increasing about 1% from the previous quarter to 54.1%, mostly as a result of favorable USD/JPY foreign exchange rates, and higher manufacturing volumes. As the company has significant manufacturing done in Japan, a weaker Japanese Yen tends to benefit profit margins, although it tends to impact P&L with a few quarters lag.

Q3 Earnings Preview

Power Integrations is scheduled to report earnings on November 6th, and according to management guidance and analyst expectations, we should see sequential improvement in revenue and profits. During the last earnings call, management guided revenues to be in the range of $110 million to $120 million, and non-GAAP gross margin is expected to slightly increase to between 54.5% and 55%. Still, the recovery appears less strong than what was previously anticipated. This is in part because some end-markets are still suffering from strong headwinds. For example, CEO Balu Balakrishnan shared during the call that appliance sales in China are lower than what the company had anticipated, in part due to the severe real estate downturn in the country.

In China, our biggest exposure is appliances. And as we mentioned, we are not seeing a pickup in demand. The inventory situation has pretty much cleared out. So we are now seeing the current demand, which is actually much lower than what we would've expected. However, thanks to design wins and also our increase in ASP due to higher power levels, we are seeing a growth that is above demand. And I think that'll continue through the second half and next year. But I think the actual raw demand is essentially flat at this point, because of all the challenges China has in terms of the real estate market.

Automotive Opportunity

Power Integrations continues to deliver design wins in electric vehicles (EVs), and expects to be in production in about 20 models by the end of this year. The company believes that the power electronics automotive opportunity could reach a billion dollars in a few years, with the company well positioned to capture a significant share with its high current GaN technology that will unlock applications with higher power levels.

GaN Opportunity

Gallium Nitride (GaN) has significant advantages compared to silicon in power electronics, and Power Integrations has significant IP in this domain. GaN offers lower on-resistance and very high-switching speeds, which has enabled smaller and more efficient power converters and motor drives. This gives the company a competitive advantage in a wide range of applications, from consumer electronics to renewable energy.

Power Integrations recently made a small bolt-on acquisition of a company called Odyssey Semiconductor, to further strengthen its GaN IP position, as Odyssey has significant expertise in vertical GaN technology, which supports even higher current than the current lateral devices. This is expected to make GaN products competitive with silicon carbide in high power applications like electric vehicle (EV) drive-train inverters. During the last earnings call, Balu Balakrishnan emphasized the importance of the GaN technology on the company's product roadmap.

So we have mentioned multiple times that most of our new products will be using GaN and they have been using GaN. And we got the best -- the first traction on cell phones, because that has much shorter design cycles. What we are seeing now is the proliferation of GaN into all of the other markets, and it's driven by our new products like InnoMux-2, GaN higher voltage inner switches with GaN. All of this are -- will be going into production over time and we see an inflection point starting in 2025. So just to give you a rough idea, next year our GaN revenue could grow as much as 50% and we see the growth rate, the slope of the growth rate, shifting starting in 2025.

Manufacturing Model

In addition to the competitive moat derived from the company's strong intellectual property and engineering know-how, it also benefits from a very unique manufacturing model. While many digital semiconductor companies like NVIDIA and AMD (AMD) have gone completely fab-less, Power Integrations partners with fabs while retaining control for much of the production process. The company develops, owns, and controls the manufacturing process of its devices, in some cases even owning key pieces of equipment, and retaining the know-how to replicate the technology process with new fab partners when needed.

Several of its fab partners are based in Japan, which is why a weaker Japanese Yen tends to benefit the company and boost gross profit margins. In the past decade, the Japanese currency has depreciated significantly, which has been a tailwind for Power Integrations. Still, this could turn into a headwind should the Japanese Yen appreciate. According to comments from management during earnings calls, it typically takes two to three quarters for the exchange rate to show in the company's financial results.

Financials

Power Integrations has significant cyclicality as a result of serving many discretionary end-markets, such as appliances, cars, and consumer devices like mobile phones and PCs. Despite this cyclicality, the company has averaged an attractive return on invested capital over the past decade of more than 12%.

To protect against this cyclicality, the company likes to maintain a fortress balance sheet to be able to continue to invest during tough economic periods and buy back shares at attractive prices. For example, Power Integrations ended the last quarter with approximately $290 million in cash and investments and basically no long-term debt.

Power Integrations started paying a dividend in 2008, and it has now grown to $0.20 per quarter. While the current dividend yield is relatively modest, the shareholder yield is quite decent once the buyback yield is added.

There is no doubt that the last couple of years have been difficult for the company, as a lot of consumer demand was pulled forward by the post-Covid boom, which resulted in shortages of products ranging from cars to appliances. This can be easily seen in the company's revenue history for the past ten years.

Still, Power Integrations has gained market share, and the structural tailwinds that benefit the company remain in place. The push for higher energy efficiency and the electrification of the economy should help growth resume its upward trajectory. As revenue recovers, so should profitability and earnings per share (EPS).

In fact, the company believes that the combination of favorable secular trends that are driving demand for advanced power chips, together with the new opportunities opened up by new GaN devices, should double the serviceable addressable market (SAM) by 2027 compared to 2022. Some of the incremental opportunities include higher content in electric vehicles (EVs), motor drives, AC-DC conversion for more efficient energy transmission, and opportunities in data center power devices. These new end-markets should also help improve mix as lower-margin mobile phone chargers and other lower-cost products come to represent a smaller percentage of revenues.

Valuation

Power Integrations has usually commanded a high premium over other analogue semiconductor manufacturers, given its growth potential and strong competitive moat. Competitors like NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) and ON Semiconductor (ON) tend to offer more commoditized devices and tend to have more mature end-markets. Analog Devices and Texas Instruments have strong competitive moats too, but these product offerings are a mix of advanced devices and some more commoditized chips as well. Given that Power Integrations is a GaN pure-play, we think the market is right to assign a premium valuation.

The 51x Price/Earnings multiple can look scary, but it is important to remember that this is probably close to the bottom of the cycle, and the company has demonstrated that it can deliver significantly higher earnings when their end-markets are healthy.

Based on analyst estimates for FY2026, the forward Price/Earnings ratio is already considerably lower, at 27x.

Based on our future earnings estimates, we calculate a net present value for the shares of roughly $60 per share when using a 10% discount rate. This is close to where shares are currently trading, implying that shares are fairly valued and priced to deliver high-single-digit to low-double-digit returns. We therefore believe shares are reasonably attractive, but would require an additional valuation margin of safety of 20% to 30% before considering them a "Strong Buy".

Risks

In the short term, the biggest risk we see is an economic recession in the U.S. or Europe that could reverse the nascent recovery for the company. Longer-term, the biggest risk we see is another competitor developing a better and cheaper alternative to GaN technology. Power Integrations has significant intellectual property and know-how invested in GaN devices, and its competitive position could quickly deteriorate should a breakthrough result in another technology outperforming GaN in cost and performance.

Conclusion

Second quarter results finally showed Power Integrations starting to recover from a prolonged cyclical downturn in many of its end-markets. Still, its fundamentals remain robust and management said during the earnings call that they have been taking market share and delivering several design wins. The company is well-placed to benefit from secular trends towards higher energy efficiency requirements, electrification of the economy, and growth in the renewable energy sector. Its technology lead in GaN semiconductors should open up several new opportunities. We believe shares are currently reasonably priced if growth expectations are met.

data.title: Cycles Have Turned Against The Bull
data.description:
The Cycles

Figure 1

The return on the last 200 trading-days of 23.11% is at the 91.7% percentile, as shown in Figure 1. The best fit sine wave indicated the best return would end on October 8th. It actually peaked August 23rd. The sine wave suggests the bull market is over. The previous bottom of the sine wave was 12/28/22 about a month and a half after the beginning of the current/recent bull market.

The sine wave currently estimates the worst 200 trading-day period in the coming cycle will end 7/16/26.

Figure 2

The 100 trading-day cycle shown in Figure 2 also indicates we are in a down phase of a cycle with an estimated bottom shown in the red label of 6/3/25.

Figure 3

The 9.4% return over the last 50 trading-days shown in Figure 3 has a percentile rank of 93.2%. This cycle like the 200 day also suggests an October top.

Figure 4

The cycle for the 25 trading-day return suggested the top would be on 10/18/24 which, since the market was down on Monday, is the current high. It’s obviously premature to call this a top, but it seems highly likely we are in a topping process that should complete soon.

Figure 5

If we haven’t had the top, perhaps it will be in by November 13th when the 12.5 trading-day sine wave shown in Figure 5 peaks.

Percentiles

Figure 6

The rose colored line in Figure 6 shows the combined percentile rank of the return for the 200 and 100 trading-day periods, currently at 90.4%. Since I last published this chart, I’ve added some purple dots to show where bull markets peaked and bear markets bottomed. The pattern leading up to the last point on the rose colored line is very similar to the pattern that led up to the bull market peak on January 3, 2022. In this previous bull market, the highest percentile peaks came during the middle of the bull, and there were lower and lower high points in the percentile rank as the bull market in the S&P 500 moved toward its highest price. While recent price action has been setting new S&P 500 highs, the momentum in sentiment appears to be weakening.

Money Supply and Stocks

Have you ever wondered if there was a downside for stocks following rapid money growth? The strongest influence of money growth on the long run stock market return appears to come with a 5-year lag time. Figure 7 shows the correlation between the 3-year growth rate of M2 with the 7-year real return on the S&P 500. M2 includes currency in circulation (like the cash in your wallet), checking account deposits, savings account deposits, retail money market funds, bank CDs and overnight repurchase agreements. The stock market return includes reinvested dividends and adjusts for CPI inflation.

Figure 7

Money growth does not have a cause and effect relationship with stocks. There can be countervailing influences. Still, slow money growth in the early 1950s corresponds with stellar stock market return in the late 1950s. The two bouts of double-digit money growth in the 1970s correspond with weak returns in the late 1970s and early 1980s. Money growth in the mid-1990s hit what at the time was the weakest since 1934. This corresponded with the peak of the dot com bubble in 2000. Strong money growth in the aftermath of the burst of the dot com bubble corresponds with the financial crisis and great recession.

Looking ahead, the monstrous growth of M2 in 2020, where the red line appears to drop straight down in Figure 7 implies a horrible bear market in stocks during 2025. The red line suggests the 7-year stock market return in January 2026 will be 0%. If that were to occur, all the stock market gains after inflation and dividends since January 2019 would be wiped out.

M2 growth peaked in March 2022 at the highest rate since WW II. This implies the bear market in stocks could last into 2027. After the coming bear market bottoms, the slowest rate of M2 growth since 1934 suggests a phenomenal bull market will ensue.

Conclusion

Even if the bear market started Monday, October 21st, sharp declines or panic selling are likely months away. I initiated a sell indicator for the SP500 with the article The Heartbeat of History is About to Thump Stocks. Even though I expect a horrible bear, the case for a strong sell isn’t there yet.

To make that call, I would want to see a decline in the market followed by a rally that fails to make a new high, and I would want to see some indication a recession had started or would start within two or three months. I believe reliable indicators show a recession is coming. I don’t yet see signs it will start within two or three months.

data.description:
Market Review

In the third quarter of 2024, equity markets were positive and small cap stocks outperformed large cap stocks. Small cap stocks were particularly strong in July as it became more apparent the Fed would start the much- anticipated rate cutting cycle at the September Fed meeting. Overall economic data continues to be mostly solid, and inflation continues to decline and is now approaching the Fed’s target level of 2%. Meanwhile, the monthly jobs’ reports softened in the middle part of the year and the unemployment rate ticked up. The combination of these datapoints led the Fed to initiate a 50bp rate cut in September as well as set expectations for continued cuts going forward. Given the market strength in 3Q and YTD 2024 and the improving breadth across small caps and the equal weighted large cap indexes, it appears investors believe the Fed has engineered a soft landing or no landing and that the US economy will avoid recession.

Third quarter performance notwithstanding, large caps have significantly outperformed for the better part of the last decade. Through 3Q 2024, the Russell 1000 Growth Index has delivered a 10-year annualized return of 16.52% vs the Russell 2000 Growth and Microcap Growth Indexes at 8.94% and 5.42%, respectively. Since the inception of our small cap and large cap track records over 25 years ago, the Russell 1000 Growth Index has delivered an annualized return of 8.48% vs the Russell 2000 Growth of 7.06%, and that includes the massive outperformance of large caps the last 10 years. With the Fed rate cut in September and combined with attractive valuations and an improving earnings outlook, we believe we could be at an inflection point where small caps start to improve on an absolute basis and outperform large caps on a relative basis.

  • There have been periods in the past when small caps outperform large caps for long periods of time. See Exhibit 1.
  • The valuation of the small cap sector relative to large caps is in the 11 th percentile historically (zero being the least expensive relatively and 100 being the most expensive). See Exhibit 2.
  • Earnings growth is expected to improve for the remainder of 2024 and 2025. See Exhibit 3.
  • Small caps have generally outperformed large caps following the first Fed rate cut and for a sustained period afterward. See Exhibit 4.

Much of this performance delta over the last decade relates to healthy business fundamentals of large cap companies, which we think can be sustained. But we also believe there is opportunity building for our smaller cap strategies.

Portfolio Review

Our investment philosophy emphasizes direct research and adhering to a strong sell discipline. We strive to own companies that we believe can grow revenue and profits at strong rates in any economic and market environment. We feel we have the opportunity, if we pick the right stocks, to perform better than the index in all market environments. However, we do not outperform in every quarter or every year, and in the third quarter, we underperformed in all strategies (net of fees). In these environments, we believe our team must remain committed to our philosophy and process. Our strategies have been tested over multiple decades and many different market and macro environments, and our team has been together through most of that time. Sticking with our discipline amidst periods of short-term underperformance has helped us outperform over the long- term in all strategies. Please see the performance table below for details.

For the quarter, our large cap strategy outperformed in financials, underperformed in industrials, health care, and consumer, and was inline in technology. In small cap and smid cap, we outperformed in technology and energy and underperformed in health care, industrials, financials, basic materials, and consumer. Our micro cap strategy outperformed in technology, consumer, and basic materials and underperformed in industrials and health care.

Market Outlook

We believe the future direction of the market will depend on the path of the economy and the direction of earnings estimates. With the first Fed rate cut in the rearview mirror, the Fed continues to lean toward a more accommodative position but is expected to closely monitor the economic data to inform future decisions. After a strong September jobs report, the expectation around the magnitude of expected rate cuts was pared back for the November and December Fed meetings. The presidential election is also being closely watched by investors. Given polls indicate a close race, we believe the market could experience some volatility leading up to and shortly after November 5 th. However, in our experience, moving past election outcomes allows the market to refocus on the path of the economy and the direction of earnings estimates. As of now, investors are expecting strong earnings growth in 2025 for small, mid and large cap companies. See Exhibit 3.

Our experienced and stable team has worked through many of these environments over the past 25 years, and we believe we can lean on our experience of bottom-up stock picking to navigate this market as well. As always, we will stay focused on our core investment philosophy. We believe a portfolio of high-quality growth companies, selected using our original research, and combined with a strong sell discipline will lead to compounding of portfolio value and market outperformance over time. We believe our since inception investment results continue to support this approach.

  • In health care, we have maintained healthy exposure where we believe fundamentals remain strong.   • Overweight in industrials driven by companies with what we believe have secular growth drivers.   • In technology, we are overweight as growth fundamentals remain solid and valuations are more attractive relative to levels seen in 2021

  • In the financial sector, we are underweight with one long-term holding in the specialty insurance area and one new regional bank holding

  • Added 13 new positions in 3Q: 4 in consumer, 3 in health care, 3 in technology, 2 in industrials, 1 in financials
